author: @AttilaAsztalos
text: Put it this way - if you had two cordless drills presented to you with the same price, but one of them had "now with a nutating gearbox!" on the box, would that make you buy that one, every time? If not, why would the industry bother? If that gearbox would mean such extra efficiency as to double battery life, then yeah sure - but as long as it's basically marginally better if at all, it doesn't matter what the _theoretical_ advantages might be...

author: @bryanseymour509
text:
I spent some time designing drills and the market pressures to shorten overall length is immense. This gear train would go a long way towards that end. I have no idea how it would manage in terms of efficiency, but I bet it would produce a hell of a lot of vibration at high RPMs.

As far as production goes, the major problem I see is with the central shaft's bearing journal for the nutating gear. That shaft is not something easily produced on existing production machine tools. It would be massively expensive to machine as a single piece. You could have the nutating journal formed by powder metallurgy and pressed onto a concentric shaft that was turned on a standard screw machine, but sintered parts dont like to be loaded in tension.  (hoop stress of the press fit) It could probably be done, but you're also placing it in an unbalanced cyclic loading situation. Eek... 

The efficiency and length benefits would need to be pretty inticing to get me to bet a production run on it.

author: @wanglydiaplt
text: Very Clever! Similar to vernier scales, yes? There's another application of the slanted disk that I've been playing with: a sort of parallel cylinder radial engine. I've seen them used as pumps and I know of one that was made to be a streamlined aircraft engine but I don't know how well it worked. I've been working on a single cylinder demo of the principal involved and it's proved to be a real 'learning experience'; that is I've reached an understanding of it that will require a complete rethink. I'll describe more when I get further along. :-)

replies.author: @jonathanedelson6733
replies.text:
I don't believe that is quite the problem with these gears. There is a variant that uses internal spur gears with high contact ratio.

IMHO the problem is that you have tremendously high pitch line velocity handling the full output torque of the system. 

The full output torque is carried by the stationary gear, the nutating gear, and the output gear.

At the same time the full input speed is seen by the contact patch zipping around the system.

This means lots of wear and low efficiency. 

You get a huge gear ratio with a single stage, but the trade is that you get lots of lost power in that stage. Think worm gear as an analogy.

A valuable tool with its place, but not a magic machine.

author: @bikerfirefarter7280
text:
It's a novel idea, compact, it could probably be easily forged/stamped/cast With a couple of smaller 'cogs' opposite the contact points, to prevent warping under heavy load, it would have huge torque output. And be much easier to maintain/adjust for wear. It looks like it would be very forgiving of contaminants/wear.
Coupled to a screw-jack it would make for very good self-leveling use of heavy machinery.
Thanks for sharing.
ATB.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i like your suggestions mate - some good thinking going on there

replies.author: @bikerfirefarter7280
replies.text:
​@ThinkingandTinkering
Thanks. Except the idler cogs would have crept around and bound up the mechanism due to the 40/41 mismatch, probably be better as rolling cones or just 'sliders': I'm probably over-thinking/engineering it though. The basic principle has a lot of possibilities, I saw something similar in a wave-plate for a torpedo engine, it had massive output torque in a compact 'cylinder', but obviously not meant to be a long life thing.
